metrics.narrative: Pain is clear but never quantified. Kristopher framed the goal as ROI on a manual Capex process ('a couple hours to procure instead of a couple weeks'; price lookups require digging through invoices). No dollar baseline, spend-under-management figure, or savings target stated — 'I'd have to see what kind of savings we could potentially get to really balance that off of the initial cost' (15 Jun). Quantifying this via the promised ROI business case is the single highest-leverage move, and it doubles as the proof that defeats the vendor-fit objection.

champion.narrative: Kristopher Nelson (VP Finance) is a developing champion: carries the CEO AI mandate, drives cadence (scheduled and proactively rescheduled the demo), engaged the Showpad content, articulated detailed pain and a 'start small, prove ROI, scale' vision. Not yet a sponsoring champion: no budget authority, no CEO intro yet, procurement (Paul) and IT (Oded Fuchs) not actively in the room, and he frames this as one of several competing priorities ('a lot of urgent needs popping up that distract', 02 Jun). Convert him by arming him with a ROI case strong enough to take upstairs.

sources: Avoma intro call 07 May 2026 (Kristopher Nelson, Steve Ovadje)
competition.narrative: The dominant competitor is do-nothing/inertia fueled by vendor-fit skepticism. Kristopher reported multiple vendors dismissed NVISION: 'JAGGAER basically off the top said you aren't a good fit... your volume is so low... you're not gonna get the ROI' (07 May). Coupa, GEP and Zip were earlier touchpoints, not an active bake-off; none are in a current evaluation. Zycus's wedge is that Steve countered the fit objection directly ('we work with SMB, mid-market and large enterprises') and positioned single-platform AI (Merlin intake, Spendminer, ANA). Win path: prove ROI on low volume with comparable healthcare-MSO references. Lose, and it dies to do-nothing.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is specific, multi-point, buyer-confirmed. Capex sourcing fully manual ('emails go out, phone calls go out... it's all outside of the system' and 'a mess to go back and audit'). Contracts are an unstructured PDF 'dumping ground'. Invoices sit in Stampley not the ERP, so price lookups are slow. No compliance controls on off-platform field purchases ('we see it when we pay the invoice'). Owner is Kristopher; day-to-day is Paul Hernandez. The open question is whether the dollar volume justifies the spend (the do-nothing risk), not whether pain exists.

sources: Salesforce account contact: Oded Fuchs, Sr Director IT
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics unexplored — no MSA, SOW, legal, procurement-channel or InfoSec discussion yet, consistent with early Formal Evaluation. Two integration dependencies (eProcurement coexisting with NV/Envy; eInvoicing with Stampley, which Kristopher flagged as 'not my world... a whole other lift') will likely trigger IT/InfoSec review before paper. Oded Fuchs (Sr Director IT) is an account contact and the natural owner of that gate. Scope the phase-1 (iSource + eProcurement) integration footprint at the demo and confirm who owns IT/security sign-off.

economic_buyer.narrative: No EB engaged. Kristopher (VP Finance, DM) deferred budget upward: 'I don't really have a targeted budget... our leadership's open to new AI tools... the price tag is a huge factor' (15 Jun). The CEO is the named AI sponsor ('our CEO is gung ho about AI, he tasked me with procurement supply chain', 07 May) but has never engaged. Budget sits above Kristopher. The correct move is to have Kristopher broker the CEO/CFO intro ONCE the ROI case is in hand — an EB intro before there's a quantified business case to present has nothing to land on.

decision_process.narrative: Self-directed, exploratory process: intro (07 May) → Showpad space (12 May) → discovery with SC (15 Jun) → demo (now 8 Jul). The purchase-approval chain for equipment is well understood (field → ops leadership → CMO >$10k → finance), but the SOFTWARE-buy decision process is unmapped: no decision date, no eval gates calendared, budget above Kristopher. The 25 Jun reschedule was a bandwidth signal ('quarter end'), not disengagement. Building a mutual close plan with Kristopher at/after the demo is the way to convert exploratory motion into a committed process.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are emerging and partly confirmed: phase-1 sourcing + eProcurement for large-ticket Capex to prove ROI before scaling ('I don't wanna just jump all in', 15 Jun); structured multi-supplier RFQ with substitute-product handling in one event (confirmed by SC Ritika); Teams-embedded intake; CMO >$10k approval gate; coexistence with NV/Envy and Stampley; ANA for sub-$10k tail spend. The overriding gate is demonstrable ROI on low volume. Scoring weights and any competitor comparison remain inferred.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 7 May, ~54 days in cycle. In Formal Evaluation, 91 days to close (29 Sep). Buyer replied 25 Jun offering two demo slots — genuine two-way engagement, not rep activity. Path from demo (8 Jul) → low-volume ROI case + references → champion-brokered EB intro → integration/InfoSec scoping → contracting fits inside a Sep close. Date is credible; risk is qualitative (unproven ROI, unmapped EB), not timing.
evidence: Kristopher Nelson proactively rescheduled the demo on 25 Jun citing quarter-end and offered two new slots (Clari email received 25 Jun) — active buyer engagement, not a stall.

Best Case is not yet defensible: no quantified ROI/business case built, no Economic Buyer engaged, exploratory buyer posture ('depends on the ROI', 15 Jun). Honest category is Pipeline until the ROI case lands and the EB is mapped.
north_star_verdict.headline: Live, well-qualified early Formal Evaluation: two strong discovery calls and a buyer who proactively rescheduled the demo to 8 Jul. 91 days is comfortable. The deal will be won or lost on one thing — proving ROI on low volume to kill the 'you're too small' narrative; the EB gap is real but downstream of that.

flags: Do-nothing is the #1 threat: vendor-fit skepticism ('too small, low volume, no ROI') is unaddressed. The 8 Jul demo must pair the AI/ANA story with a concrete low-volume ROI model and 2-3 comparable healthcare-MSO references — this is the single highest win-probability lever.

EB unmapped but downstream: Kristopher deferred budget to leadership and the CEO (AI sponsor) has never engaged. Route the EB ask THROUGH Kristopher once the ROI case exists — an exec intro with nothing to present wastes the one shot at power.

Single-threaded: only 2 buyer contact roles; Paul Hernandez (day-to-day procurement) attended but did not engage, and IT (Oded Fuchs) is silent. Activate Paul at the demo and pull IT in to de-risk the NV/Envy + Stampley integration review.

No mutual close plan: exploratory posture with no decision date. Post-demo, build dated gates (ROI delivery → EB intro → integration/InfoSec → contracting → close) to convert motion into a committed process inside the Sep window.
best_practice_check.summary: Engaged early-stage deal with a developing champion, unmapped power and an unproven ROI. The win sequence is: (1) prove low-volume ROI + references to kill the do-nothing narrative, (2) arm Kristopher to broker the EB intro on the back of that case, (3) multi-thread into procurement and IT.

Realistic timeline is 8 to 10 weeks from demo (25 Jun) to close, landing mid-to-late Sep 2026.

Position Merlin intake + ANA (autonomous negotiation agent) as the AI-driven differentiation aligned with CEO AI mandate that reduces manual lift and delivers measurable procurement efficiency gains across 100 locations.
